- Severities: Minor, Moderate, Major, OutOfService
- Driver status: Active, Probation, Inactive, Terminated, OnLeave
- Unit status: InService, OutOfService, InMaintenance, Sold, Totaled
- Equipment type: DryVan, Reefer, Flatbed, Tanker, Container, Chassis, StepDeck, Lowboy

- Vendor type: Fuel, Repair, Tires, Towing, Insurance, Permit, Toll, Other
- Location type: Yard, Customer, Vendor, TruckStop, Other
- Equipment-log event type: AssignedToUnit, RemovedFromUnit, LocationChanged, InspectedOK, DamagedReported, LostReported, Retired

# IH35 TMS / ERP — Master Blueprint v3

**Project:** IH 35 Transportation LLC — integrated TMS + ERP + driver mobile PWA
**Owner:** Jorge Munoz · Laredo, TX · ~50 trucks operating · ~70 drivers · Chapter 11 DIP
**Document version:** v3 — clean rebuild from scratch on a modern stack
**Document status:** canonical source of truth · supersedes v1 and v2 · supersedes Cursor's `IH35_NEW_SYSTEM_BLUEPRINT_V1.*`
**Companion document:** `IH35_CURSOR_BUILD_SPECIFICATION.md` (the *how*; this document is the *what*)

---

## DOCUMENT CONTROL

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Version | v3.0 |
| Date | 2026-05-02 |
| Authors | Jorge Munoz (owner) · Claude (architect/specifier) |
| Reviewers | Cursor (build agent — must read top to bottom before any code) |
| Supersedes | v1 (`IH35_MASTER_BLUEPRINT.md` 1,497 lines); v2 (`IH35_MASTER_BLUEPRINT_v2.md` 2,267 lines); `IH35_NEW_SYSTEM_BLUEPRINT_V1.md` (Cursor parallel draft) |
| Related | `IH35_PRE_BUILD_QUESTIONNAIRE.docx` (questionnaire answers Jorge returned) · `IH35_SESSION_REGISTER.md` (build log) · 12 approved UI section preview screenshots |
| Update rule | Any change to a "MUST" or "INVARIANT" requires Jorge's written approval before edit. Other changes update the Change Log at the bottom. |
| Voice | Plain English. No frameworks-of-the-month. No jargon Jorge cannot read at midnight. |

### Status tags used in this document

- **MUST** — non-negotiable; failing this requirement means the build is broken
- **SHOULD** — strongly recommended; deviating requires written justification in the build log
- **MAY** — optional; build team's discretion
- **PHASE 2** — explicitly deferred; not built in v1 but designed-around so it slots in cleanly later
- **INVARIANT** — a system-wide rule that holds at all times; violating it breaks the offline story, the audit story, or the financial integrity story

### How to read this document

1. **Read Parts 0-3 in full** before reading anything else. They contain decisions that govern every other part. Skipping them and starting at Part 7 (the sections) means you will misimplement the sections.
2. **Read Parts 4-6** before starting any module. They contain the data model, integrations, and design system that every section depends on.
3. **Each section in Part 7** is a self-contained build target. Within a section: read the UI spec, then the business logic, then the API contract, then the validation rules.
4. **Cross-section workflows (Part 8)** are end-to-end stories that span multiple sections. Read them once you've read the sections involved.
5. **Appendix** is reference; consult as needed.

---

# PART 1 — EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

## 1.1 What this software is

A single integrated business management system that replaces the current patchwork of QuickBooks Online, Samsara fleet console, Relay fuel cards, paper logs, separate damage trackers, separate driver apps, separate maintenance trackers, and the existing 37,000-line `maintenance.html` web tool with **one coherent product** that runs the entire IH 35 Transportation operation.

The system has three users surfaces, one backend, one database, and four external integrations. Everything written in our system is mirrored to QuickBooks (the source of truth for accounting in v1). Every fleet event is mirrored from Samsara (the source of truth for HOS, GPS, and DTC codes). Every fuel transaction is mirrored from Relay (the source of truth for fuel-card data). Bank transactions flow in through QuickBooks's bank feed in v1, and through Plaid directly in Phase 2.

## 1.2 The problem this software solves

A 50-truck (growing to 200+) trucking company in Chapter 11 DIP cannot afford manual reconciliation between operations and accounting. Every operational event — a fuel purchase, a roadside breakdown, a driver settlement, a load delivery, an accident, a leave request — must automatically produce the correct financial record (bill, expense, deduction, invoice, journal entry) without any office staff re-keying data.

The current patchwork has four breaking failure modes:

1. **Things drop on the floor.** A driver reports an in-transit issue by phone; nobody opens a work order; the truck gets back to the yard with the same problem. The damage isn't recorded. The bill never gets created. The driver's deductible is never collected.
2. **The financial record drifts from the operational record.** A fuel purchase happens on Relay; the office posts an expense to QBO; nobody links the two; the same fuel gets expensed twice. Or once. Or zero times. Form 425C goes out wrong.
3. **The Chapter 11 monthly operating report is built by hand each month** from Excel exports. It takes 2-3 days. It is the legal document under penalty of perjury that keeps the bankruptcy case alive. Manual = error = risk.
4. **Edits to the system are not visible.** Someone changes a driver's pay rate at 11pm; settlement runs at 6am; nobody knows who changed it; driver disputes; trust collapses.

The new system solves all four by enforcing **system-wide invariants** (Part 11) backed by a **module-bounded modular monolith** (Part 2) where every operational event automatically generates its corresponding financial event through an **outbox pattern** that survives external-system outages.

## 1.3 The five tightly-coupled domains

1. **Operations** — work orders, maintenance, damage reports, fuel logs, dispatch loads, in-transit issues, roadside inspections
2. **Driver finance** — settlements, deductions, escrow, cash advances, debt tracking, signed acknowledgments
3. **Accounting** — bills, expenses, bill payments, invoices, customer payments, journal entries, vendor/customer/item/category master data, full chart of accounts (mirrored to QBO in v1)
4. **Banking** — DIP banking, transaction categorization, reconciliation, factoring as virtual bank, Form 425C reporting
5. **Driver mobile** — offline-first PWA for HOS, load acceptance, fuel logging, BOL upload, in-transit issue reporting, settlement viewing, signed-document acceptance

These five are not independent. A roadside breakdown in Domain 1 (Operations) creates a work order in Domain 1, a bill in Domain 3 (Accounting), a possible driver deduction in Domain 2 (Driver finance), a payment in Domain 4 (Banking), and a notification to the driver in Domain 5 (Driver mobile). All five must transact atomically — either all happen or none happen — or the system tells the truth about what is incomplete.

## 1.4 Who uses it

| Role | Surface | Access | Headcount |
|---|---|---|---|
| Owner (Jorge) | Office web app | Everything; Manager actions auto-emailed | 1 |
| Administrator | Office web app | Everything except DELETE | 1-2 |
| Manager | Office web app | Everything; "suspicious" edits auto-emailed to Owner | 1-2 |
| Accountant | Office web app + Reports | Banking · Accounting · Reports · 425C · Maintenance read | 1-2 |
| Dispatcher | Office web app | Dispatch · Maintenance · Create expenses (no banking, no accounting) | 2-4 |
| Safety officer | Office web app | Dispatch · Safety · Reports · Maintenance · WO · Bills (no financials, no bank balances) | 1-2 |
| Driver | Driver PWA only | Own data + own submissions | ~70 (growing to 200+) |
| Mechanic | Office web app | Maintenance only | 2-4 |

## 1.5 Core value proposition

**Every operational event automatically produces the correct financial record without re-entry.**

Concrete examples:

- Driver hits "Report issue" in the PWA → creates an In-Transit Issue record → if dispatch converts to Repair Work Order → WO creates a Bill in Accounting (vendor = repair shop) → Bill creates a Journal Entry (debit Repairs, credit AP) → if driver liable, creates a Driver Deduction (credit AR-driver, debit Driver Liability) → Deduction posts on next settlement → settlement creates Bill Payment → Bill Payment debits AP, credits Bank → 425C line 21 (cash disbursements) updates → all of this happens with one click and all of it is auditable.
- Fuel purchase on Relay → mirrors to our DB as Fuel Transaction → matched to a Load → Load's Driver Bill auto-updates fuel reimbursement → Settlement reflects fuel expense → expense category = "Diesel" → Class = unit number → mirrored to QBO → P&L per truck updates → 425C lines 21 (disbursements) + line 33 (cash disbursements actual) update → IFTA quarterly preparer sees gallons by state.
- Driver pre-trip on PWA flags brake defect → creates Defect record → notifies Safety + Maintenance → if defect is OOS, creates urgent Repair WO → blocks dispatch from assigning unit until WO closed → unit status reflects in Dispatch board → Office sees in real time on big screen.

The office staff is freed from being data-entry clerks. They become managers.

## 1.6 What lasts 100 years vs. what gets replaced

This system is designed for a 100-year horizon. Over that horizon every external SaaS will be acquired, repriced, deprecated, or replaced. The architecture protects against that:

- **What lasts:** the data model (Part 4), the business logic (Part 7), the API contracts (Part 5 + Appendix), the audit trail (Part 11). These are owned, written down, and portable.
- **What gets swapped:** every external integration lives behind a thin adapter layer (Part 5). QuickBooks gets replaced by our own accounting in Phase 2 by swapping one adapter. Samsara gets replaced if needed by swapping its adapter. Relay too. Bank feeds switch from QBO to Plaid by swapping the bank-feed adapter.
- **What scales:** Postgres handles billions of rows on a single instance well-tuned. The modular monolith scales by splitting modules into services later if ever needed (Part 2.4). Most likely it never needs to be split.

The single most important architectural decision in this document is **Option C+** (Part 2.5): we build our full chart of accounts and journal entries from day one as a *shadow ledger* alongside QuickBooks. QuickBooks is the source of truth for accounting in v1, but every transaction also writes to our own accounting tables. This means: when we replace QuickBooks in Phase 2, we already have years of real accounting data in our own DB to operate from. The replacement is a flip of one switch, not a rebuild.

## 1.7 Scope boundaries

**In scope for v1:**
- Office web app (Sections 1-11 of Part 7)
- Driver PWA (Section 12 of Part 7)
- All integrations (QBO, Samsara, Relay; bank feeds via QBO)
- Full master data layer (vendors, customers, items, categories, classes, accounts)
- Full journal entry shadow ledger (Option C+ Part 2.5)
- Form 425C with auto-fill from real lines per the official 12/17 form
- IFTA quarterly preparer
- Document storage with 5-year retention
- Audit log with who/when/before/after/IP/session_id
- Notification matrix (SMS + email + in-app)

**Out of scope for v1, designed-for-Phase 2:**
- AI image damage comparison (UI mockups exist; logic deferred)
- Direct bank feeds via Plaid (QBO bank feed is the v1 source)
- Multi-currency (USD only in v1; peso later)
- Replacing QBO with our own accounting engine (the data model is reserved; the engine is Phase 2)
- Live Loves API integration (manual Excel upload + Relay matching is v1)
- Native mobile apps (PWA covers iOS + Android in v1)

**Permanently out of scope:**
- Payroll processing (handled by external payroll provider; we feed it data)
- Tax filing (handled by external accountant; we provide reports)
- Driver recruiting (handled by external; we onboard once hired)
- Customer-facing portal (Phase 3 if ever)

## 1.8 Success criteria for v1 launch

The v1 system is considered successful and ready to replace the current system when, on a typical operational day, all of the following hold true without human intervention:

1. A driver receives a load assignment in the PWA and accepts it offline; data syncs when online; office sees acceptance in real time.
2. The driver's Driver Bill is created the moment the load is dispatched, with PC*Miler-calculated paid miles, deadhead miles, and fuel-card linked expenses.
3. A fuel purchase on Relay shows up in the system within 1 hour, matched to the correct driver, unit, and load, with the correct expense category and class.
4. A roadside breakdown reported in the PWA appears on the Maintenance In-Transit Issues panel within 30 seconds, notifies dispatch + safety, and can be converted to a Work Order in one click that automatically creates the corresponding Bill.
5. A settlement run at end of week shows the driver's loaded miles, deadhead miles, deductions (with debt alert), escrow balance, and net pay; signs digitally; mirrors to QBO; updates Form 425C.
6. Form 425C for the prior month auto-fills 80%+ of all lines from system data; only owner-projection lines (35-37) require manual entry.
7. IFTA quarterly preparer pulls miles from Samsara and gallons from Relay+Loves+dispatch and produces the IFTA-ready CSV with one click.
8. The system continues to operate when QuickBooks is offline; pending-sync KPI shows on dashboard; sync resumes when QBO returns.
9. The audit log shows every state change with who, when, before, after, IP, session_id; Manager edits to financials send an automatic email to Owner.
10. The 425C for the month reconciles within $1 against the QBO P&L for the same period.

---

# PART 2 — ARCHITECTURE

## 2.1 The architectural shape (one paragraph)

A **modular monolith** built in **TypeScript on Node.js** running on **Render**, backed by a **single PostgreSQL database on Neon**, deployed from a **single Git repository**, with **three frontends** (office web app in React + Vite, driver PWA in React + Vite + Workbox + IndexedDB, public reports in React) sharing the same backend, and **four external integrations** (QuickBooks Online, Samsara, Relay, bank feeds via QBO in v1 / Plaid in Phase 2) each behind their own **adapter layer** so any one of them can be swapped in Phase 2 without touching the other 11 modules.

## 2.2 Why modular monolith and not microservices

You asked "should this be one big thing or different parts each with its own database?" The honest answer for your size and growth horizon is **one big thing internally separated into hard-bounded modules**. Here is why, in plain English.

**Microservices** means: each module runs as its own server, with its own database, communicating with other modules over HTTP/gRPC over a network. This is what Amazon, Uber, and Netflix do. The benefit is scaling each module independently and isolating failures. The cost is enormous: 12 separate servers to deploy, 12 databases to back up, distributed transactions that fail in 50 different ways, network calls that go wrong silently, monitoring 12 services instead of 1, and a DevOps team you don't have.

**Modular monolith** means: one server, one database, one deploy, but inside the codebase the modules are rigidly separated. Each module owns its database tables. Modules talk to each other only through defined contracts. A module cannot reach into another module's tables directly — the code prevents it. From the outside it looks and ships like one application; from the inside it has the same boundaries microservices give you, without the network and DevOps overhead.

For your size — 50 trucks growing to 200, ~600 loads a month, ~5,000 transactions a month — Postgres on a single Neon instance handles this with two orders of magnitude of headroom. Stripe processed billions of dollars on a single Postgres for years. Shopify runs the largest e-commerce platform in the world on a modular monolith today. Your transaction volume will not approach the limits of a single Postgres in your lifetime.

The previous agent who recommended microservices was protecting you from the right thing — one giant unstructured codebase is hard to fix — but the prescription was wrong for your scale. The modular monolith gives you the same protection (modules can't reach into each other) with 1/10th the operational complexity.

**If we ever do outgrow this**, splitting one module out into its own service is a 2-3 week project per module, because the boundaries are already drawn. We don't pay that cost until we need it, and we probably never will.

## 2.3 The 12 modules

Each module owns a **schema namespace** in PostgreSQL (`identity.*`, `master_data.*`, etc.). Each module exposes a set of **API endpoints** under `/api/{module}/...`. Modules can read other modules' data only through **defined views** or **service contracts**. Modules cannot write to other modules' tables, ever.

| # | Module | Schema | Owns | Reads from |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identity & RBAC | `identity` | users, sessions, roles, permissions, IP allowlist | (none) |
| 2 | Master Data | `master_data` | vendors, customers, drivers, units, trailers, items, categories, classes | Identity (audit) |
| 3 | Catalogs & Lists | `catalogs` | safety/maint/dispatch/fuel/drivers/fleet/accounting catalogs (the per-domain lookup tables) | Master Data |
| 4 | Dispatch & Loads | `dispatch` | loads, load_legs, load_stops, load_status_history, driver_bill_drafts | Master Data, Identity |
| 5 | Driver Finance | `driver_finance` | settlements, presettlements, deductions, advances, escrow_movements, signed_acknowledgments | Dispatch, Master Data, Accounting |
| 6 | Maintenance | `maintenance` | work_orders (4 types), wo_lines, in_transit_issues, defects, parts_inventory | Master Data, Safety, Accounting |
| 7 | Safety & Compliance | `safety` | accidents, dot_inspections, antidoping_records, complaints, civil_fines, internal_fines, permits, insurance_policies, claims | Master Data, Drivers |
| 8 | Fuel | `fuel` | fuel_transactions, route_plans, route_recommendations, fuel_compliance | Master Data, Dispatch |
| 9 | Accounting | `accounting` | bills, bill_payments, expenses, invoices, customer_payments, journal_entries, gl_postings, accounts | Master Data |
| 10 | Banking | `banking` | bank_accounts, bank_transactions, bank_reconciliations, factoring_movements, transfers, cc_payments | Accounting |
| 11 | Documents | `documents` | document_metadata, document_versions, document_links | (cross-cuts everything) |
| 12 | Notifications | `notifications` | notification_events, notification_deliveries, notification_preferences | Identity |

Two **shared services** that every module uses but no module owns:

- **Audit log** (`audit.events`) — every state change anywhere in the system writes here. Immutable. 5-year retention.
- **Outbox** (`outbox.queue`) — every external sync goes through here. Background workers drain it.

## 2.4 What each module looks like inside

Every module has the same internal layout. This is enforced by linter rules in the codebase.

```
src/modules/{module-name}/
  domain/         ← business rules, no I/O
    entities.ts
    rules.ts
    state-machines.ts
  data/           ← Postgres I/O via Drizzle ORM
    schema.ts
    repositories.ts
  api/            ← HTTP endpoints (Fastify routes)
    routes.ts
    validators.ts
  events/         ← outbox publishers, event subscribers
    publishers.ts
    subscribers.ts
  index.ts        ← module's public contract: what other modules can call
```

The `index.ts` exports the module's public surface. Anything not exported there is private. A linter rule prevents any other module from importing anything except through `index.ts`. This is what "module boundary" means in code.

## 2.5 The Option C+ data model in one paragraph

Build the **full chart of accounts**, **full journal entries table**, **full vendors/customers/items/categories/classes tables**, and **full bills/bill_payments/expenses/invoices/customer_payments tables** in our own Postgres database from day one. QuickBooks remains the **primary source of truth** for accounting in v1 — every transaction posts to QBO first via the QBO adapter — but every transaction also writes to our own corresponding tables in parallel. We become a **shadow ledger**: every QBO journal entry has a corresponding row in our `accounting.journal_entries` table with full debit/credit detail. A nightly reconciliation routine compares QBO's account balances to ours; mismatches surface in a dashboard. Phase 2, when we replace QBO, we already have years of real journal entries in our own DB; we flip the QBO adapter from "primary" to "export-only" or remove it; our DB becomes primary; nothing else changes. **What we defer to Phase 2:** the calculation engine on top of journal entries — period closing, P&L compilation, balance sheet compilation, 1099 generation, tax line mapping. v1 reads those from QBO; Phase 2 we compute them ourselves from our own journal entries. Net effect: we pay ~10% extra in v1 build cost to save ~70% in Phase 2 replacement cost, and we get nightly QBO-vs-ours reconciliation as a free side effect.

## 2.6 The technology stack — every choice and why

Each row of this table is locked. Cursor MUST NOT substitute an alternative without written approval.

| Layer | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Backend language | TypeScript | Same language as frontend (one team can fix anything); compiler catches type errors before they hit production; official TypeScript SDKs for QBO, Samsara, Plaid, Stripe |
| Backend runtime | Node.js 22 LTS | Long-term support until 2027; broad library support; matches existing system |
| Backend framework | Fastify 5.x | Fastest Node web framework; built-in JSON schema validation; first-class TypeScript |
| ORM | Drizzle ORM | TypeScript-native; generates SQL you can read; no magic; lightweight |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 on Neon | Already paid for; supports schemas (per-module isolation); supports branching for dev environments; scales to billions of rows |
| Background jobs | BullMQ on Redis | Industry standard for Node; reliable retries; supports the outbox pattern; cheap on Render |
| Frontend office app | React 19 + Vite + TanStack Query + Tailwind CSS | React = the only large frontend ecosystem with 10+ years of runway; Vite = fastest dev server; TanStack Query = best server-state library; Tailwind = matches our locked design tokens |
| Frontend driver PWA | React 19 + Vite + Workbox + IndexedDB (via Dexie) + service worker | Same React stack so one codebase; Workbox = Google's production-grade PWA toolkit; IndexedDB = real offline storage; Dexie = ergonomic wrapper |
| Frontend reports | React 19 + Vite + TanStack Table + Recharts | Same as office app; TanStack Table for the heavy report grids |
| File storage | Cloudflare R2 (S3-compatible API) | $15/TB/month vs AWS S3 $23/TB/month; **zero egress fees** (S3 charges to read your own files); 5-year retention is cheap |
| Hosting | Render (web + workers) + Neon (DB) + Cloudflare R2 (files) + Upstash (Redis) | All services we already use or are S3-API-compatible; ~$50-80/month total at v1 launch scale |
| Realtime updates | Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Simpler than WebSockets; works through corporate firewalls; auto-reconnect; sufficient for our needs |
| Error monitoring | Sentry (free tier in v1) | Industry standard; alerts on uncaught exceptions; sourcemap support |
| Uptime monitoring | UptimeRobot (already have) | Two monitors per environment: `/` and `/api/health` |
| Test framework | Vitest + Playwright | Vitest for unit tests; Playwright for E2E; both maintained by Vite ecosystem |
| Auth provider | Lucia Auth (self-hosted) | Email + password + IP allowlist in v1; SSO in Phase 2; no SaaS lock-in |
| SMS provider | Twilio | Industry standard; Programmable Messaging API; pay per message |
| Email provider | Resend | Modern, deliverability-focused; React Email for templates |

## 2.7 Repository layout

One Git repository. One deploy. One source of truth. Layout:

```
└── LICENSE
```

This is a **monorepo** managed by **npm workspaces**. One `npm install` at the root sets up everything. CI/CD runs against the root.

## 2.8 Hosting topology

| Component | Service | Tier in v1 | Approx cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backend API + workers | Render Web Service (Starter or Standard) | Standard ($25/mo) for production after launch; Starter ($7/mo) for dev | $7-25/mo |
| PostgreSQL database | Neon (existing project, new branch) | Free tier covers v1 launch volume; upgrade as needed | $0-19/mo |
| Redis | Upstash (free tier) | Free for v1; pay-as-you-go later | $0-10/mo |
| File storage | Cloudflare R2 | ~$15 per TB/month, $0 egress | $5-20/mo |
| Office web app | Render Static Site | Free | $0 |
| Driver PWA | Render Static Site | Free | $0 |
| Reports app | Render Static Site | Free | $0 |
| Sentry | Sentry free tier | Free for low volume | $0-26/mo |
| UptimeRobot | Existing free | Free | $0 |
| Twilio | Pay per message | ~$0.0083/SMS | ~$15/mo at v1 volume |
| Resend | 3,000 emails/mo free | Free in v1 | $0-20/mo |

**Total v1 monthly hosting**: approximately $50-100/month at launch, scaling linearly with growth. The current `ih35-dispatcher.onrender.com` ($25/mo) and Neon project remain running in parallel during the build, so combined hosting during the 3-4 month build window peaks at ~$75-150/mo.

## 2.9 Environment strategy

Three environments, each fully isolated:

| Env | Purpose | Postgres branch | Render service | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Local | Cursor + developers run locally | `localhost` Docker Postgres | n/a | `localhost:3000` |
| Staging | Jorge tests new features before they go live | `staging` branch on Neon | `ih35-erp-staging` Render | `staging.ih35-erp.com` |
| Production | Live system used by drivers and office | `production` branch on Neon | `ih35-erp-prod` Render | `app.ih35.com` (or whatever domain Jorge picks) |

Branching is automatic on Neon (branches are zero-cost copies). Render auto-deploys from Git: pushes to `main` deploy to staging; tags to `release-*` deploy to production.

## 2.10 Observability

Three layers of visibility into what the system is doing:

1. **Logs** — every API request, every background job run, every external API call logged structured JSON. Aggregated in Render's log stream. Searchable.
2. **Errors** — every uncaught exception sent to Sentry with stack trace, breadcrumbs, user context. Sentry emails Owner immediately on production errors.
3. **Metrics** — request rates, response times, queue depths, sync lag, outbox backlog all exposed at `/metrics` endpoint (Prometheus format). Render scrapes and displays.

A **system health dashboard** at `/admin/health` shows in real time: QBO connection status, Samsara connection status, Relay connection status, outbox depth, last successful nightly reconciliation, current Sentry error rate, current request rate, slowest endpoints. This is the page Jorge or the on-call person opens at 2am when something feels off.

## 2.11 Backup and disaster recovery

| Asset | Backup method | Frequency | Retention | RPO | RTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PostgreSQL | Neon point-in-time recovery + nightly `pg_dump` to R2 | Continuous + nightly | 30 days PITR + 5 years cold | <1 min | <30 min |
| Documents (R2) | R2 versioning + cross-region replication | Continuous | 5 years per Q17 retention | <1 min | <15 min |
| Source code | Git on GitHub + local clones | On every push | Forever | 0 | 0 |
| Secrets | Render env vars + 1Password backup | On change | Forever | 0 | <5 min |

**RPO** (recovery point objective) = how much data can we afford to lose. **RTO** (recovery time objective) = how long can we be down. The targets above mean: in the worst case (Neon goes down completely), we lose at most 1 minute of data and we're back online within 30 minutes by restoring from `pg_dump` to a fresh Postgres.

---

# PART 3 — CROSS-CUTTING CONCERNS

This part answers the 10 architectural gaps Cursor flagged. Each concern is a system-wide decision that affects every module. They are all **MUST** unless marked otherwise.

## 3.1 Source of truth per domain

Every entity in the system has exactly one **system of record** (SoR). All other systems are mirrors or derivations. Writing to a mirror without updating SoR is forbidden. This rule is enforced in code by the adapter layer.

| Entity | System of record (v1) | Mirrors / derivations | System of record (Phase 2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vendor / Customer | **Our DB** + QBO (dual-write; our DB is authoritative on conflict) | (none) | Our DB |
| Driver master profile | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO as Vendor; mirrored to Samsara driver list | Our DB |
| Unit (truck, trailer) | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO as Class; mirrored to Samsara as Vehicle | Our DB |
| Item / part | **Our DB** + QBO (dual-write) | (none) | Our DB |
| Chart of accounts | **QBO** in v1; **our DB** Phase 2 | Our DB shadow | Our DB |
| Journal entries | **QBO** in v1; **our DB** Phase 2 | Our DB shadow ledger | Our DB |
| Bills | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO | Our DB |
| Bill payments | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO | Our DB |
| Expenses | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO | Our DB |
| Invoices | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO | Our DB |
| Customer payments | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO | Our DB |
| Bank accounts | **QBO** in v1; **our DB** Phase 2 | Our DB shadow | Our DB |
| Bank transactions | **QBO bank feed** in v1; **Plaid** Phase 2 | Our DB | Our DB |
| Hours of Service | **Samsara** | Our DB cache | Samsara |
| GPS / location | **Samsara** | Our DB cache | Samsara |
| DTC codes | **Samsara** | Our DB cache | Samsara |
| Fuel transactions (Relay) | **Relay** | Our DB | Relay |
| Fuel transactions (Loves Excel) | **Our DB** (uploaded) | (none) | Our DB |
| Loads | **Our DB** | (none) | Our DB |
| Driver bills | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO as bills | Our DB |
| Settlements | **Our DB** | Mirrored to QBO as bill payments | Our DB |
| Work orders | **Our DB** | (none; bills are mirrored) | Our DB |
| Damage reports | **Our DB** | (none) | Our DB |
| Documents | **R2 (file)** + **our DB (metadata)** | (none) | Same |
| Audit log | **Our DB** | (none) | Same |

**INVARIANT 3.1.1** — Every write to a non-SoR system happens *after* the SoR write succeeds. Order is enforced via the outbox.

**INVARIANT 3.1.2** — When SoR and a mirror disagree (detected by nightly reconciliation), the SoR wins. The mirror is corrected. The audit log records the correction.

## 3.2 Canonical ID strategy

Every entity in the system has a single canonical ID that never changes. External systems may have their own IDs; we store those as foreign references but never use them as primary keys.

**MUST 3.2.1** — Every primary key is a UUIDv7 generated server-side. UUIDv7 is time-ordered (sortable by creation), making it nearly as efficient for indexing as a sequential ID while remaining globally unique and not exposing record counts.

**MUST 3.2.2** — Every entity that mirrors to QBO stores `qbo_id` (text) as a unique foreign reference. NULL means "not yet synced." Stored alongside `qbo_synced_at` timestamp.

**MUST 3.2.3** — Every entity that mirrors to Samsara stores `samsara_id`. Same rules.

**MUST 3.2.4** — Every entity that mirrors to Relay stores `relay_id`. Same rules.

**MUST 3.2.5** — Human-readable identifiers (load numbers like `L-13501`, work order numbers like `WO-088`, settlement numbers like `STL-2026-04-001`) are generated by domain logic, stored as a separate `display_id` field, and indexed unique within their type. They are user-visible; UUIDs are not.

**MUST 3.2.6** — Display IDs are never reused. Once `L-13501` exists, no other load gets that number, even if `L-13501` is later voided.

## 3.3 Accounting posting map

For every event in the system that affects money, there is a defined posting rule. This is the spine of Option C+.

**MUST 3.3.1** — Every accounting event creates one journal entry. A journal entry is a balanced set of debits and credits. Sum of debits = sum of credits, always.

**MUST 3.3.2** — Every posting references: (a) date, (b) account, (c) debit OR credit amount (never both), (d) memo, (e) class (= unit number where applicable), (f) source event UUID (the event that caused the posting).

**MUST 3.3.3** — Posting rules are defined as code in the Accounting module's `domain/posting-rules.ts`. They are versioned. Changing a rule requires a migration, not just a code change.

**MUST 3.3.4** — When QBO is the SoR for accounting (v1), our journal entry is created **after** QBO confirms the posting succeeded. The QBO journal entry ID is stored on our row.

The posting rule catalog (abbreviated; full version in Appendix):

| Event | Debit account | Credit account | Class | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bill created (vendor expense) | (per category line) | Accounts Payable | (per unit / split) | Standard AP entry |
| Bill payment | Accounts Payable | Bank account | (none) | Reduces AP, reduces cash |
| Driver expense reimbursable on settlement | Driver Reimbursable Expense | (per source: bank or AP) | (per unit) | Tracked separately for settlement clearing |
| Driver settlement (gross pay) | Driver Pay (per type) | Accounts Payable - Driver | (per unit) | Settlement = bill from driver vendor |
| Driver settlement (deductions) | Accounts Payable - Driver | (per deduction account) | (per unit) | Reduces what driver is paid |
| Driver settlement (escrow withholding) | Accounts Payable - Driver | Driver Escrow Liability | (per unit) | Held money |
| Driver escrow payout | Driver Escrow Liability | Bank account | (none) | When driver leaves with positive escrow |
| Driver fine/damage liability created | Driver Receivable | Driver Liability Income (or Repair Recovery) | (per unit) | Driver owes us |
| Driver liability collected via settlement | Accounts Payable - Driver | Driver Receivable | (per unit) | Settlement pays AP and clears AR |
| Cash advance to driver | Driver Receivable | Bank account | (none) | Driver now owes us |
| Cash advance settled (collected) | Accounts Payable - Driver | Driver Receivable | (none) | Settlement clears the advance |
| Customer invoice (load) | Accounts Receivable | Trucking Income | (per unit) | Standard AR entry |
| Customer payment | Bank account | Accounts Receivable | (none) | Money in |
| Factoring sale of invoice | Factoring Reserve + Factoring Fee Expense + Bank Account | Accounts Receivable | (per unit) | Sells AR for cash + held reserve |
| Factoring chargeback | Accounts Receivable | Factoring Reserve | (per unit) | Customer didn't pay; we owe back |
| Factoring reserve release | Bank Account | Factoring Reserve | (none) | We get the held cash back |
| Bank transfer | (target bank) | (source bank) | (none) | Internal transfer |
| Credit card payment | Credit Card Payable | Bank account | (none) | Pays down CC |
| Fuel purchase (Relay) | Diesel Fuel Expense | Credit Card Payable (Relay) | (per unit) | Card transaction |
| Fuel purchase (driver paid, reimbursable) | Diesel Fuel Expense | Driver Reimbursable Expense | (per unit) | Settlement pays driver back |
| Insurance allocation across units | Insurance Expense (per unit) | Insurance Prepaid (or AP) | (per unit) | Monthly amortization split |

**MUST 3.3.5** — Every posting rule is unit-tested with a sample event and the expected debits/credits.

**MUST 3.3.6** — A posting rule that produces an unbalanced journal entry (debits ≠ credits) is rejected before write. The system halts the event and writes an audit error.

## 3.4 Settlement closing rules

Settlements are the most legally and emotionally sensitive operation in the system. A driver disputes settlement → trust collapses → driver leaves → operation suffers.

**MUST 3.4.1** — A settlement has four states: `draft`, `presettlement`, `final`, `voided`.
- `draft` = system is gathering data; editable
- `presettlement` = first load closed, more loads expected; editable; not yet paid
- `final` = locked; paid; immutable
- `voided` = canceled before payment; reason required; auditable

**MUST 3.4.2** — A settlement transitions `presettlement → final` only when: (a) all loads in the settlement are status `delivered` with documents uploaded, (b) all driver-claimed expenses are reviewed, (c) all deductions for this period are applied, (d) the debt-alert disclosure has been acknowledged by the user signing off, (e) the digital signature exists on file.

**MUST 3.4.3** — A `final` settlement cannot be edited. To correct an error, void the original (`final → voided`) and issue a corrected one (`draft` → `final`). Both remain on file. The audit log links them.

**MUST 3.4.4** — Only Owner or Administrator can void a `final` settlement. Manager cannot. Voiding sends an immediate email to Owner.

**MUST 3.4.5** — Voiding triggers a reversing journal entry that exactly offsets the original. Both entries remain on the books.

**MUST 3.4.6** — A driver whose settlement was voided gets a notification through the PWA explaining what happened, with the new corrected settlement attached when ready.

**MUST 3.4.7** — Settlement period is the calendar week (Sun-Sat) by default, but **per-driver overrideable** via driver profile (some drivers go on per-load settlement; some on bi-weekly).

**MUST 3.4.8** — A settlement can include loads from prior weeks if those loads were not yet "back home" (the trip wasn't complete). The system uses **trip completion** not week boundary as the default rule.

**INVARIANT 3.4.9** — Debt alert: at the moment a settlement is shown to anyone (owner, accountant, dispatcher, driver), the driver's total debt to the company is shown at the top of the screen, in red, before any payable amount. This is non-negotiable per Q1.3.

## 3.5 Permission matrix at action level

Roles are coarse. Permissions are fine. Every API endpoint and every UI button checks a specific permission, not just a role.

**MUST 3.5.1** — Permissions are encoded as strings of the form `module.entity.action`, e.g. `accounting.bill.create`, `accounting.bill.delete`, `driver_finance.settlement.void`, `safety.dot_inspection.read`.

**MUST 3.5.2** — Each role is mapped to a set of permission strings. The mapping is in `identity/role-permissions.ts` and is loaded at startup. Changing it requires a code change + deploy, not a runtime config flip.

**MUST 3.5.3** — Every protected endpoint declares its required permission. Fastify checks it before the handler runs. Missing permission → 403.

**MUST 3.5.4** — Every protected button/menu/screen in the UI checks the user's permission set client-side and hides the control if absent. (Server-side check is the source of truth; client-side hide is UX only.)

The full permission matrix appears in Part 9. Highlights:

- **Administrator** has every permission EXCEPT any `*.delete` permission. They can void/archive but not delete.
- **Manager** has every permission, but every Manager action that creates/modifies a financial record (`accounting.*` or `banking.*` or `driver_finance.*`) writes a flag to the audit log that triggers an email to Owner.
- **Dispatcher** has `dispatch.*`, `maintenance.*`, `master_data.read`, `accounting.expense.create`, but NOT `accounting.bill.*`, NOT `banking.*`, NOT `accounting.invoice.*`.
- **Safety** has `safety.*`, `dispatch.*`, `maintenance.*`, `accounting.bill.create`, `accounting.expense.create`, but NOT `banking.*`, NOT `accounting.payment.*`, and NO read access to `banking.account.balance`.
- **Driver** has `driver_pwa.*` only — a separate scope that cannot touch any office endpoint.
- **Mechanic** has `maintenance.work_order.*` and `maintenance.parts_inventory.*` only.

## 3.6 Notification matrix

Every event that should notify someone is declared in a single notification catalog. Adding a new notification means adding a row, not adding code in 3 places.

**MUST 3.6.1** — Each notification has: trigger event, recipient role(s), channel(s), urgency, retry policy.
**MUST 3.6.2** — Channels: `app_push`, `email`, `sms`, `desktop_toast`. Urgency: `critical`, `routine`, `info`.
**MUST 3.6.3** — Critical = SMS + email + app push, retried on delivery failure with backoff for 1 hour.
**MUST 3.6.4** — Routine = email + app push, retried on delivery failure for 15 minutes.
**MUST 3.6.5** — Info = app push only, no retries.
**MUST 3.6.6** — Recipient can opt out of routine and info per channel via preferences page. Cannot opt out of critical (the system needs to reach them).

The full matrix is in Part 10. Sample rows:

| Event | Recipients | Channel | Urgency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driver reports in-transit issue | Dispatcher, Safety, Owner | SMS+email+push | critical |
| Driver pre-trip flags OOS defect | Safety, Maintenance, Dispatcher | SMS+email+push | critical |
| Accident reported | Safety, Owner, Insurance Coordinator | SMS+email | critical |
| QBO connection lost | Owner, Administrator, Manager | email | critical |
| Settlement ready for review | Accountant, Owner | email | routine |
| New load booked | Driver assigned | app push | info |
| Permit expiring in 30 days | Safety, Owner | email | routine |
| Permit expiring in 7 days | Safety, Owner | email+SMS | critical |
| Manager edited a financial record | Owner | email | routine |

## 3.7 Offline conflict rules

The driver PWA is offline-first. The driver edits records offline; office may edit the same records online. When the driver comes back online, conflicts must resolve cleanly.

**MUST 3.7.1** — Every offline-editable entity has a `version` integer field. Every read returns the current version. Every write includes the version the writer is editing against.

**MUST 3.7.2** — On sync, server compares submitted version to current version. Match → write succeeds, version increments. Mismatch → conflict.

**MUST 3.7.3** — Conflict resolution is **field-level last-write-wins** for non-financial fields (status, notes, photos), with the loser's version preserved in audit log.

**MUST 3.7.4** — Conflict resolution is **office-wins** for financial fields (pay rate, deduction amount, settlement total). Driver's offline edit is rejected with explanation; driver sees a notification.

**MUST 3.7.5** — Conflict resolution is **manual review** for status fields where both sides submitted a transition (driver said "delivered," office said "voided"). System holds both, surfaces in a "needs review" inbox, blocks settlement until resolved.

**MUST 3.7.6** — Driver PWA stores all pending writes in IndexedDB. Pending count shows in PWA top bar at all times. Pending writes survive app restart, phone reboot, and uninstall+reinstall (within 7 days via cache-restore endpoint).

## 3.8 Document taxonomy

Per Q7, all documents are cross-filed and searchable. The metadata schema must support that.

**MUST 3.8.1** — Every document has these fields: `uuid`, `filename`, `mime_type`, `size_bytes`, `r2_key`, `uploaded_by_user_id`, `uploaded_at`, `category` (enum), `links` (array of {entity_type, entity_uuid}), `version`, `superseded_by_uuid` (nullable), `legal_hold` (boolean), `retention_until` (date).

**MUST 3.8.2** — Categories enum: `bol`, `pod`, `rate_confirmation`, `dispatch_instructions`, `accident_report`, `damage_photo`, `dvir`, `dot_inspection`, `antidoping_result`, `medical_card`, `cdl`, `permit`, `insurance_policy`, `claim`, `signed_acknowledgment`, `vendor_invoice`, `bank_statement`, `tax_form`, `legal_doc`, `other`.

**MUST 3.8.3** — Same physical file can be linked to multiple entities (one signed acknowledgment may link to a Driver, a Settlement, and an Accident). The `links` array supports this; each entry is a separate row in `documents.links` table.

**MUST 3.8.4** — Versioning: when a document is superseded, the old version is kept; new version's `superseded_by_uuid` points to old. Search returns latest by default; filter shows versions.

**MUST 3.8.5** — Legal hold: while `legal_hold = true`, the document cannot be deleted regardless of retention policy. Legal hold can only be set/cleared by Owner.

**MUST 3.8.6** — Retention: default `retention_until = uploaded_at + 5 years` per Q17. Documents past retention with no legal hold are flagged for deletion in a quarterly review queue. Owner approves the batch deletion. Even after approval, deletion is logged.

**MUST 3.8.7** — Search is full-text on filename + OCR text (extracted on upload via Tesseract for PDFs and images). Filters: category, date range, linked entity, uploader, version status.

## 3.9 SLA and non-functional requirements

| Concern | Target | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| API response time (read endpoints, p95) | < 300 ms | Office staff perception of "instant" |
| API response time (write endpoints, p95) | < 800 ms | Tolerates one DB roundtrip + one outbox write |
| Page load (office app, p95) | < 2 s | Modern web norm |
| Page load (driver PWA, p95 on 4G) | < 3 s | Mobile + cellular tolerance |
| Background sync lag (Samsara HOS) | < 60 s | "Live HOS" must be live enough for dispatch decisions |
| Background sync lag (QBO bills/expenses) | < 5 min | QBO API is rate-limited; not real-time |
| Background sync lag (Relay fuel) | < 60 min | Relay polls daily; we poll hourly |
| Uptime | 99.5% | ~3.6h/month tolerable; ~7.2h/month not |
| Data durability (RPO) | < 1 minute | Neon point-in-time recovery |
| Recovery time (RTO) | < 30 minutes | From `pg_dump` restore tested quarterly |
| Concurrent users | 50 | 20 office + 70 drivers, not all simultaneous |
| Database size at v1 launch | < 5 GB | Comfortable on Neon free tier |
| Database growth | ~1-2 GB/year | Within Neon paid plan |
| Document storage at v1 launch | ~50 GB | ~$1/month on R2 |
| Document growth | ~100 GB/year | Within reasonable R2 cost |

## 3.10 Cutover and migration plan

The current `ih35-dispatcher.onrender.com` runs. Drivers depend on it. Office depends on it. We cannot turn it off until v2 is fully tested.

**Cutover phases:**

| Phase | Duration | What happens | Success criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Build v1 modules | 12-16 weeks | Cursor builds module by module per Part 12 build order; each module merged to staging | All sections pass acceptance tests; staging mirrors production data |
| 2. Parallel run | 4 weeks | Production data flows into v1 staging through nightly mirror; office staff trained on staging; no driver impact | Office staff comfortable; <5 critical bugs/week |
| 3. Driver pilot | 2 weeks | 5 volunteer drivers use v2 PWA; old PWA still works | Pilot drivers report no functional regressions |
| 4. Cutover weekend | 1 weekend | Old system frozen Friday 6pm; final reconciliation; v2 goes live Sunday 6am | All loads/expenses reconcile within $1 |
| 5. Old system read-only | 6 months | Old system stays online, read-only, for historical reference | No writes; archive frozen |
| 6. Old system retired | At month 6 | Final `pg_dump` archived; old Render service deleted | Archive verified; cost ends |

**Historical data migration:**

Per Q17 (5-year audit retention), historical data carries forward. Migration scripts in `scripts/migration/` convert each entity:

- Vendors / customers / drivers / units / trailers — copied as-is from current DB; `qbo_id` preserved
- Loads from current dispatch — copied; status preserved; documents re-linked
- Bills / expenses / payments since 12/01/2024 (per Q3 — bankruptcy filing date) — copied
- Bank transactions since 12/01/2024 — copied
- Factoring history since 12/01/2024 — copied per Q3
- Driver settlements since 12/01/2024 — copied
- Audit log — old system's audit becomes initial audit entries in new system, marked `migrated: true`
- Documents — copied to R2; metadata recreated; OCR re-run
- Maintenance work orders — copied with state preserved
- Damage reports — copied with state preserved

**No journal entries are migrated**: QBO is the source of truth for v1 accounting, so the journal entry shadow ledger starts populating only with transactions made *after* cutover. Pre-cutover journal entries continue to live in QBO and are read from there for any historical report. Phase 2's QBO replacement project includes a separate one-time migration of historical journal entries from QBO into our DB.

## 3.11 Logic source traceability (mandatory)

This is the section Cursor proposed and I am locking in as **MUST**.

**MUST 3.11.1** — Every requirement in this document is tagged with its source. Sources are:

- `[Q]` — questionnaire (Jorge's answers in `IH35_PRE_BUILD_QUESTIONNAIRE.docx`)
- `[UI]` — approved UI section preview (one of the 12 widgets we built and Jorge approved)
- `[v2]` — carried forward from `IH35_MASTER_BLUEPRINT_v2.md`
- `[legacy]` — extracted from current production system (`maintenance.html`, `apps/fleet-reports-hub`, backend routes)
- `[infer]` — reasonable inference where source is silent; review before relying
- `[new]` — introduced in v3 by architect; written justification in this document

**MUST 3.11.2** — In Parts 4-12, every section header carries one or more source tags in brackets. Parts 1-3 (this part and earlier) are entirely `[new]` because they are architectural framing, but each cross-cutting concern in Part 3 references the questionnaire question it answers (e.g., 3.10 ↔ Q3, Q17).

**MUST 3.11.3** — When something is `[infer]`, the inference is stated explicitly so Jorge can reject it. Example: "v3 infers settlement period defaults to calendar week (Sun-Sat) [infer]; Q1.3 said weekly/per-load/custom-quantity options without specifying default. Confirm before build."

This makes audit-trail-of-the-spec possible: when something breaks 6 months from now, anyone can trace why a feature exists by reading the tag.

# END OF PARTS 1-3 — CHECKPOINT

This is where I stop for your review.

**Parts 1-3 contain every decision that downstream parts depend on:**
- What this software is and the success criteria
- The modular monolith architecture, the 12 modules, the stack
- Option C+ data model in summary form (full detail in Part 4)
- Source-of-truth, IDs, posting rules, settlement closing, permissions, notifications, offline conflicts, documents, SLAs, cutover, traceability

If anything here is wrong, downstream is wrong. So please read carefully.

Reply with one of:

- **approve** — I write Parts 4-6 next (Data Model, Integrations, Design System)
- **change X** — tell me specifically what to change
- **add Y** — tell me what's missing

The full v3 will be ~3,500-4,500 lines when finished. Parts 4-6 are the most data-heavy parts (every table, every adapter contract, every design token). Parts 7-13 are the section-by-section UI + business logic (carried mostly from v2 with the Option C+ corrections). Then the build spec, then the screenshots.

# IH35 Master Blueprint v3 — Part 3 Extension · Batch A

**Sections covered:** 3.12 QBO Auto-Sync + Offline Queue/Replay · 3.13 Driver Liability/Deduction/Escrow Accounting · 3.14 Unit Cost Allocation Engine

**Reading order:** read after Parts 1-3 (sections 3.1 through 3.11). Batch B (sections 3.15 through 3.18) follows after this batch is approved.

**Normative language:** MUST · MUST NOT · SHOULD · SHOULD NOT · MAY (RFC 2119 conventions). Every requirement that affects financial integrity, audit, or external system contracts is **MUST** unless explicitly marked **SHOULD**.

**WF coverage in this batch:** WF-008, WF-009, WF-011, WF-012, WF-014, WF-022, WF-031, WF-032, WF-033, WF-034, WF-035, WF-036, WF-045, WF-046, WF-064 (partial — full coverage continues in Part 7 module specs)
